Okay, let me try to figure this out step by step. 

So, I'm looking at the problem where a mutation was made in the ZonedChronology class of Joda-Time. The mutant changed some code lines 121-127. After this change, several tests started failing, and one test actually passed after being fixed.

First, I'll look at what the mutant did. From the provided diff, it seems that the equals method was altered. Originally, the equals method checked both the base and zone of the chronology. But in the mutant, they removed checking the base and only check the zone now. So line 123-125 changed from checking getBase().equals(chrono.getBase()) to just returning whether the zones are equal.

Now, why would this cause issues? Well, the equals method is supposed to determine if two objects are equal. If two ZonedChronology instances have the same zone but different bases (like different calendar systems), they should not be considered equal because their behavior could differ in how dates are handled.

Looking at the failing tests: several TestDateTimeZoneCutover tests were still failing after the mutation, meaning that the equals change didn't fix them. However, one test, testPropertyGetMonthOfYear in TestMonthDay_Properties, started passing because of the mutation. This suggests that perhaps the base wasn't being checked correctly before and fixing it helped that specific case.

But when the equals method stopped checking the base, some other parts of the code might have been relying on this check. For instance, serialization tests like TestSerialization were failing because their expected objects weren't matching anymore—probably due to the equals/hashCode not properly considering all necessary fields.

So, the main issue here is that by removing the base from the equals method, we've introduced inconsistencies elsewhere in the code where objects are compared or serialized. The hashCode also changed, but without seeing it, I can assume that if the equals doesn't check 'base', the hashCode might not align correctly either.

Given all this, the mutant caused a lot of tests to fail because other parts of the system rely on ZonedChronology's equality checks considering both base and zone. Therefore, reverting the change to include the base in equals would likely fix those failing tests.
